expectations.text: Explicitly states the loop invariant as a logical predicate
expectations.evidence: Section 3 formally states loop invariant I as: '(∀x ∈ [lo, i): x <= pivot_value) ∧ (∀x ∈ [i, j): x > pivot_value) ∧ (arr[hi] == pivot_value) ∧ (lo <= i <= j <= hi)' with universal quantifiers and logical conjunctions.

expectations.text: Shows that the loop body maintains the invariant
expectations.evidence: Section 3 'Maintenance' provides detailed case analysis: Case 1 (arr[j] <= pivot) shows why swap preserves segments, Case 2 (arr[j] > pivot) shows why skipping preserves sorted segments. Both branches prove I holds after iteration.

expectations.text: Provides a bounding function (variant) that decreases on each iteration
expectations.evidence: Section 4 'Termination Proof' explicitly defines t(j) = hi - j, proves it's always positive during loop (hi - j > 0 while j < hi), and proves strict decrease: t(j+1) = hi - (j+1) = t(j) - 1 < t(j).

expectations.text: The partition function is correct and would work in quicksort
expectations.evidence: Implementation uses Lomuto scheme correctly. Post-loop swap places pivot at final position. Postcondition satisfied: [lo,i) all ≤ pivot at arr[i], [i+1,hi+1) all > pivot. Test suite in Section 5 covers basic, all-less, all-greater, mixed, subrange, duplicates cases.

expectations.text: Implementation is in Python as requested
expectations.evidence: Entire implementation in Section 2 is Python 3: uses for-loop, if/else, array swaps, docstrings, and comments consistent with Python syntax and idioms.
